narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at their academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 22 (well under the usual 36.70), Year 1 decile rank of 2 (compared to an average of 5.71), Year 3 decile of 5 (close to the average 5.53), middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 45% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. Among all the factors considered, your Year 3 school decile ranking had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass because it showed solid mid-pack performance relative to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which significantly pulled the prediction in the opposite direction—toward failure—due to how far below average it was, signaling potential challenges with the rigorous reasoning demands of law school and the bar. Your middle family income bracket played a smaller but still helpful role, nudging things slightly toward a passing prediction, as students from similar backgrounds have shown decent bar success rates in the data. Overall, while your full-time enrollment plan aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%), it wasn't a major driver here; the low LSAT outweighed your stronger Year 3 standing and income stability. Compared to typical students who get approved, your LSAT stands out as a bigger hurdle, as higher scorers consistently predict better bar outcomes in the model's view. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many from similar backgrounds overcome these metrics through targeted preparation. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, as that could flip the prediction dramatically; consider GPA-boosting post-bac courses or community college honors programs; and explore law schools with holistic reviews or bar passage support programs tailored to diverse applicants like yourself. Building a stronger academic narrative now will open doors—persistence pays off for students in your position. Keep pushing; your Year 3 improvement shows you have the capacity to rise.
